1: autos de egō Paulos parakalō hymas dia tēs prautētos kai epieikias tou Christou: 'I myself, Paul, appeal to you by the meekness and gentleness of Christ.' The emphatic autos de egō (I myself, Paul) marks a sharp rhetorical pivot — the personal-signature opening that breaks from the 'we' of chs. 1-9. Paul invokes Christ's prautēs (meekness/gentleness, the character of the lowly servant) and epieikeia (reasonableness/fairness/gentle equity — the quality of yielding one's strict legal rights for relational harmony; cf. Phil 4:5). The opponents characterize Paul as tapeinos (lowly/humble — pejorative: spineless when present) but tharrōn (bold) when absent.
2: deomai de to mē parōn tharrēsai tē pepoithēsei hē logizomai tolmēsai epi tinas tous logizomenous hēmas hōs kata sarka peripatountas: 'I beg of you that when I am present I may not have to show boldness with such confidence as I count on showing against some who suspect us of walking according to the flesh.' Kata sarka peripatein (walk according to the flesh) — the opponents' charge that Paul's ministry operates by ordinary human power-strategies. Pepoithēsis (confidence, from peithō — persuade/trust) — Paul has a settled confidence about his apostolic authority, which he would prefer to exercise through gentle persuasion rather than direct confrontation. The conditional appeal contains an implicit threat.
3: en sarki gar peripatountes ou kata sarka strateuometha: 'For though we walk in the flesh, we are not waging war according to the flesh.' The careful distinction: en sarki peripatein (walk in the flesh) acknowledges Paul's physical-human existence; kata sarka strateuomentha (wage war according to flesh) denies that his apostolic warfare uses fleshly weapons. The distinction is between the sphere of existence (unavoidably physical) and the standard of operation (not humanly strategized). Strateuomai (wage war, serve as a soldier) introduces the military metaphor that dominates vv. 3-6 — a sustained campaign analogy for apostolic confrontation of false teaching.
4: ta gar hopla tēs strateias hēmōn ou sarkika alla dynata tō theō pros kathairesin ochrōmatōn: 'For the weapons of our warfare are not of the flesh but have divine power to destroy strongholds.' Hopla (weapons, instruments of warfare) — used earlier in 6:7 for the 'weapons of righteousness'; here more broadly for all apostolic means. Dynata tō theō (powerful for God/through God) — the dative tō theō is the 'dative of reference' expressing divine enabling (cf. 'mighty before God,' Acts 7:20). Kathairesin (tearing down, demolition) from katheireō (pull down, destroy) — the same word used of pulling down buildings; the strongholds (ochyromata) are intellectual-spiritual fortifications, not physical structures.
5: logismous kathairountes kai pan hypsōma epairomenon kata tēs gnōseōs tou theou kai aichmalōtizontes pan noēma eis tēn hypakoēn tou Christou: 'We destroy arguments and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God, and take every thought captive to obey Christ.' Logismoi (arguments/reasonings/calculations, from logizomiai) — abstract intellectual constructs that resist the gospel. Hypsōma epairomenon (height raised up, lofty opposition) — the spatial military metaphor of elevated fortification. Aichmalōtizō (take captive as prisoner of war) applied to noēma (thought/intention, from noeō, think) — the mind is the battlefield; every thought must be made a prisoner of war and brought under Christ's lordship. The military vocabulary is relentlessly spatial and physical.
6: kai en hetoimō echontes ekdikēsai pasan parakoēn hotan plērōthē hymōn hē hypakoē: 'being ready to punish every disobedience, when your obedience is complete.' Ekdikēsai (punish, execute justice against, from ekdikeō) — administrative justice exercised within the community. The sequence is crucial: hymōn hē hypakoē plērōthē (your obedience is complete) must come first; then the judicial action against the disobedient. This protects the innocent majority from apostolic discipline aimed at a factional minority. Parakoē (disobedience, from parakouō = hear beside/past) is the lexical opposite of hypakoē (obedience, from hypakouō = hear under/attentively).
7: ta kata prosōpon blepete ei tis pepoithen heautō Christou einai touto logizesthō palin eph heautou: 'Look at what is before your eyes. If anyone is confident that he is Christ's, let him remind himself that just as he is Christ's, so also are we.' Ta kata prosōpon (the things before the face, 'what is in front of you') — an idiom for what is plain, visible, obvious. Christou einai (to be Christ's) — belonging to Christ as one's ultimate allegiance; the opponents apparently claimed exclusive or superior Christological standing. Paul's response is symmetrical: if they are Christ's, so are we. The possessive is not an elite status but a universal Christian identity.
8: ean te gar kai perissoteron ti kauchēsōmai peri tēs exousias hēmōn hēs edōken ho kyrios eis oikodomēn kai ouk eis kathairesin hymōn ouk aischynthēsomai: 'For even if I boast a little too much of our authority, which the Lord gave for building you up and not for tearing you down, I will not be ashamed.' Exousia (authority, power, right) — the apostolic authority given by the Lord for oikodomē (building up, edification, construction). The telological constraint on authority: it exists for building-up (eis oikodomēn), not tearing down (eis kathairesin). Kathaireō (demolish) is the same word used in v. 4-5 of demolishing strongholds — apostolic authority that demolishes intellectual obstacles does not thereby demolish people.
9: hina mē doxō hōs an kataplēssein hymas dia tōn epistolōn: 'so that I may not appear to be frightening you with letters.' Kataplēssō (strike down with terror, frighten, from kataplēttō) — the opponents apparently claimed Paul's letters were designed to intimidate in a way his physical presence could not back up. This verse explains the qualification of v. 8: Paul boasts of authority but does not want that boast to be read as mere epistolary intimidation from a safe distance. The letter/presence dichotomy (v. 10-11) is the opponents' weapon against Paul's authority.
10: hoti hai men epistolai phēsin bareiai kai ischyrai hē de parousia tou sōmatos asthenēs kai ho logos exouthemenenos: 'For they say, 'His letters are weighty and strong, but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech of no account.'' The opponents' critique is quoted. Bareiai kai ischyrai (weighty and strong) — the letters are granted rhetorical force; the critique is about the person, not the writing. Asthenēs (weak, feeble) applied to Paul's parousia tou sōmatos (bodily presence) — physical presence and rhetorical performance. Exouthemenenos (held in contempt, treated as nothing, from exoutheneō) applied to logos (speech) — his actual delivery was reportedly unimpressive; ancient forensic rhetoric required commanding physical presence and delivery.
11: touto logizesthō ho toioutos hoti hoioi esmen tō logō di epistolōn apontes toioutoi kai parontes tō ergō: 'Let such a person understand that what we say by letter when absent, we do when present.' Tō logō ... tō ergō (in word ... in deed) — the consistency claim: the written authority and the present action will match. This is a direct challenge to the opponents' letter/presence dichotomy: Paul's ergon (deed/action) when present will correspond to his logos (word/speech) in letters. The challenge is implicitly that the opponents' impressive presence will prove hollow when measured against apostolic deeds.
12: ou tolmōmen gar enkrinai ē synkrinai heautous tisin tōn heautous synistanontōn: 'Not that we dare to classify or compare ourselves with some of those who are commending themselves.' Enkrinō (classify, enroll in a group) and synkrinō (compare, measure against) — both verbs of comparative evaluation. Heautous synistanontōn (those who commend themselves) — the opponents' self-promotion, contrasted with the divine commendation Paul will claim in v. 18. Heautous en heautois metrountes kai synkrinomtes heautous heautois: 'measuring themselves by themselves and comparing themselves with themselves' — the solipsistic folly of self-referential evaluation: a measuring rod that can only measure itself.
13: hēmeis de ouk eis ta ametra kauchēsometha alla kata to metron tou kanonos hou emerisen hēmin ho theos metrou: 'But we will not boast beyond limits, but will boast only with regard to the area of influence God assigned to us, to reach even to you.' Ametra (beyond measure, without limits) — the opponents' boasting exceeds proper boundaries. Kanōn (rule, measuring line, standard) — in Greek the kanōn was literally a straight rod used in carpentry/masonry; extended to a rule, standard, or sphere of authority. Paul's boasting stays within the kanōn (sphere/territory/rule) that God assigned him — geographically determined by the Gentile mission mandate of Gal 2:7-9.
14: ou gar hōs mē ephiknoumenoi eis hymas hyperekteinomen heautous kai gar mechri hymōn ephthasamen en tō euangeliō tou Christou: 'For we are not overextending ourselves, as though we did not reach you. For we were the first to come all the way to you with the gospel of Christ.' Hyperekteinō (overextend, stretch beyond measure, from hyper- + ekteinō) — NT hapax; Paul is not stretching his authority into territory not assigned to him. Ephthasamen (we arrived, we reached, from phthanō = to come before, precede) — Paul arrived first. The claim of first-arrival establishes the territorial right: Corinth is within Paul's assigned kanōn because he was the pioneer missionary there.
15: ouk eis ta ametra kauchōmenoi en allotriois kopois elpida de echontes auxanomenēs tēs pisteōs hymōn en hymin megalynthēnai kata ton kanona hēmōn eis perisseian: 'We do not boast beyond limit in the labors of others. But our hope is that as your faith increases, our area of influence among you may be greatly enlarged.' Allotrioi kopoi (labors of others) — the opponents boast in founding work not their own; Paul refuses to count Corinth as his achievement when the founding is being claimed by interlopers. Auxanomenēs tēs pisteōs hymōn (as your faith grows) — the Corinthians' maturation in faith enables Paul's mission to expand further westward (toward Rome and Spain, Rom 15:23-24). The Corinthians' growth is the launching-pad for further pioneering.
16: eis ta hyperekeina hymōn euangelisasthai ouk en allotriō kanoni eis ta hetoima kauchēsasthai: 'so as to preach the gospel in lands beyond you, without boasting of work already done in another's area of influence.' Hyperekeina (beyond, the regions beyond) — Paul's missionary ambition extends past Corinth toward the western Mediterranean. Allotrios kanōn (another's rule/territory) — repeating the kanōn vocabulary: Paul will not claim credit for work done in someone else's assigned territory. The geographical mission ethics of the early church: apostles respected each other's founding territories and did not plant on already-founded ground (cf. Rom 15:20: 'ambitious to preach where Christ has not already been named, so that I do not build on someone else's foundation').
17: ho de kauchōmenos en kyriō kauchasthō: 'Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord.' A near-citation of LXX Jer 9:22-23 (= 9:23-24 ET): 'do not let the wise boast in their wisdom, nor the mighty in their might, nor the rich in their riches; but let him who boasts boast in this, that he understands and knows me' — applied to the kyrios (Lord = Christ in Paul's usage). The OT citation is Paul's final word on the boasting dispute: all legitimate boasting is re-centered on the Lord, not on apostolic prowess, territory, or impressive performance. The same citation appears in 1 Cor 1:31 — a recurring Pauline rhetorical move against all human glory-competition.
18: ou gar ho heauton synistation ekeinos estin dokimos alla hon ho kyrios synistēsin: 'For it is not the one who commends himself who is approved, but the one whom the Lord commends.' Dokimos (approved, tested and found genuine, the outcome of dokimazō) is the opposite of adokimos (disqualified, 1 Cor 9:27). The self-commendation critique: ho heauton synistatōn (the one who commends himself) — the opponents' self-promotion cannot produce genuine dokimotēs. Only the Lord's commendation counts as authentic approval. The closing verse of ch. 10 frames the entire apostolic debate (1:1-10:18) in the register of divine evaluation: apostolic legitimacy is determined at the Lord's judgment seat, not by community reception or rhetorical display.
